problem.title: Design a URL Shortener
problem.brief: Design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Users submit long URLs and get back a short slug; anyone who visits that slug later gets redirected to the original URL.
problem.opening_prompt: Here's the problem: design a URL shortener like bit.ly. Users submit long URLs and get back a short slug; anyone who visits that slug later gets redirected to the original URL. Take it from there.

sections.label: Requirements Clarification
sections.goal: Lock a requirements contract with functional requirements, NFRs, and explicit in/out scope
sections.objectives: Explicit in/out scope list; at least one NFR (latency, availability, or consistency) agreed; read/write ratio discussed
good_signals: Asks clarifying questions before proposing architecture
Identifies p99 redirect latency as the dominant SLO unprompted
Raises read/write asymmetry as a structural constraint
Proposes scale estimates before touching design
weak_signals: Lists only functional requirements, no NFRs
Does not ask about scale at all
sections.faang_bar: Senior IC drives the requirements themselves. Quantifies redirect QPS. Names cold-link long-tail as a constraint. Ties every NFR to an architectural implication before drawing anything.

exit_gate.description: At least one quantitative or non-functional signal before leaving requirements. Contract must be locked.

signals.description: Named latency, availability, consistency, or durability needs
Named read/write asymmetry as a constraint
RED: Could not propose requirements without prompting
RED: Started designing before requirements were agreed

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Bounds scope with in/out list when prompted. Names 1-2 NFRs. Asks about scale when asked.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Drives requirements list themselves. Quantifies redirect QPS at hundreds of thousands per second. Names cold-link long-tail. Ties NFRs to architecture.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Surfaces abuse risk and link rotation policy unprompted. Frames SLO in cost-per-redirect. Names explicit out-of-scope with rationale.

sections.label: High Level Design
sections.goal: End-to-end design covering all required breadth components from the requirements contract
sections.objectives: Write API and redirect service separated; caching layer on read path; storage choice justified; analytics counter designed; TTL handling addressed
good_signals: Separates write API from redirect service
Places cache in front of DB on redirect path
Justifies storage choice against read/write ratio
Considers CDN or edge caching for redirect path
Designs analytics counter separately from redirect path
weak_signals: Monolithic service for reads and writes
sections.faang_bar: Candidate covers all required breadth components. Justifies storage choice. Separates concerns between write and redirect. Explains caching strategy with TTL reasoning tied to link expiry.

exit_gate.description: At least one substantive HLD signal collected. All required_breadth_components addressed at minimum.

signals.description: Separate services or paths for write vs redirect
Cache correctly positioned on redirect hot path
Analytics counter decoupled from redirect path

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Draws write API and redirect service separately. Names a cache. Picks storage but doesn't deeply justify.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Covers all breadth components. Justifies storage against read/write ratio. Designs cache with TTL reasoning. Decouples analytics from critical path.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Reasons about CDN edge caching unprompted. Identifies cold-start problem for new links. Async write path for click counting. Thinks about geo-distribution.

sections.label: Deep Dive
sections.goal: Depth on the topics that make this problem interesting: ID generation and the redirect critical path
sections.objectives: ID generation strategy named with collision handling; redirect path latency characterized end-to-end; horizontal scaling approach for ID generation

deep_dive_topics.label: ID / Slug Generation
deep_dive_topics.description: How are short slugs generated? How are collisions handled? Does the strategy scale horizontally without coordination?
deep_dive_topics.what_good_looks_like: Concrete strategy (not just 'hash the URL'). Explicit collision handling. Horizontally scalable without a global coordination bottleneck. Custom slug collision handled with first-write-wins semantics.

deep_dive_topics.label: Redirect Critical Path
deep_dive_topics.description: What happens end-to-end when a user visits a short URL? Where is latency introduced at each hop?
deep_dive_topics.what_good_looks_like: Traced hop-by-hop with latency estimates at each step. Cache hit vs miss paths distinguished. p99 characterized.

deep_dive_topics.label: Scaling the Read Tier
deep_dive_topics.description: How does the redirect service scale to 500k/sec? What are the bottlenecks?
deep_dive_topics.what_good_looks_like: Cache hit rate reasoning. DB read replica or KV store justification. CDN placement. Identifies where the first bottleneck is.

exit_gate.description: At least one deep dive topic explored with depth.

signals.description: Concrete ID generation approach with reasoning
Redirect path traced end-to-end with latency
Read tier scales without coordination bottleneck

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Names an ID generation approach. Addresses collision at a high level. Describes redirect path at component level.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Horizontally scalable ID allocator with collision probability reasoning. Custom slug first-write-wins semantics. Redirect path traced with per-hop latency estimates.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Birthday paradox implications for slug collision. ID strategy without global coordination. Long-tail cold-link problem and its impact on cache hit rate.

sections.label: Reliability & Operations
sections.goal: Define the redirect SLO, how it's monitored, and what happens when dependencies fail
sections.objectives: Concrete SLO metric named; monitoring signal described; failure behavior and fallback articulated

exit_gate.description: At least one operations signal collected.

signals.description: Failure behavior and fallback explicitly described

one_down.label: SDE-2 / Mid (L4)
one_down.description: Names an SLO. Mentions monitoring. Describes basic failure behavior.

target.label: Senior SDE (L5)
target.description: Concrete redirect SLO tied to p99 latency target. Specific alert signal and threshold. Degraded-mode behavior when cache or DB fails, with explicit fallback.

one_up.label: Principal / Staff (L6)
one_up.description: Connects SLO to business impact. Error budget and burn rate alerting. On-call runbook structure. Capacity planning and traffic forecasting as part of operations.

fault_scenarios: Your primary database just went down mid-traffic spike. Walk me through what happens end-to-end.
Cache miss rate just spiked to 80%. What degrades first in what you've described?
A user reports their short link is redirecting to the wrong URL. How do you debug that in production?
Your slug generation is returning collisions at 10x the expected rate. What's happening and how do you recover?
A high-profile link is being used for a phishing campaign. What does your system do, and how fast?
raise_stakes_prompts: How would you explain the cost model for this system to your VP — specifically the cost per redirect?
You're now supporting this service in 5 geographic regions. What breaks first in your current design?
Three other product teams are building features on your API. How does that change your design decisions?
Your slug namespace is at 80% capacity. How do you handle that operationally?
You need 99.999% availability on the redirect path. What does that mean architecturally and operationally?
variant_scenarios: Now assume 90% of your traffic is automated bots, not human clicks. How does your design change?
New requirement: links must be editable after creation. What breaks in your current design?
Your service is now the infrastructure for a public URL shortener API — third parties are generating links. What changes?
You need to support link previews — before redirecting, show the destination and a safety warning. How does that affect your architecture?
